regulator: MAS (Monetary Authority of Singapore) -- simultaneously Singapore's central bank and its single integrated financial regulator, with no separate central-banking or monetary authority; sole regulator of this pillar. IRAS (Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore) has a real but strictly ancillary role limited to the GST and income-tax treatment of digital tokens, not monetary policy, CBDC issuance, or prudential bank regulation, which sit with MAS alone.
instruments: MAS media release, 'MAS Lays Foundation for Safe and Innovative Use of Digital Money in Singapore', Singapore, 16 November 2023 -- unveils three initiatives: (i) publication of the Orchid Blueprint, setting out the technology infrastructure -- a settlement ledger, a tokenisation bridge connecting account-based settlement systems to tokenised-money ledgers, a Purpose Bound Money (PBM) programmability protocol, and a Name Service for wallet-address readability -- needed to facilitate digital-money transactions; (ii) expansion of Project Orchid's industry trials, including a tokenised-bank-liabilities trial first run at the Singapore FinTech Festival 2023; and (iii) a plan to 'commence the development of CBDC for wholesale interbank settlement' the following year, piloting the 'live' issuance of wholesale CBDC for the first time after MAS had previously only simulated issuance in test environments (footnoting that the earlier Project Ubin had issued only a simulated wholesale CBDC, 'not available to the general public'); states verbatim that MAS is promoting three forms of digital money -- 'wholesale CBDCs, tokenised bank liabilities, and regulated stablecoins' -- defining wholesale CBDCs as 'issued only to financial institutions for the settlement of inter-bank transactions and represent a direct liability of the central bank', and tokenised bank liabilities as 'tokens on a ledger that represent a commercial bank's liability'; quotes then-Managing Director Ravi Menon describing the planned 'live' CBDC issuance as 'a significant milestone in MAS' digital money journey that began in 2016'; confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF via the Singapore Government's official press-release repository (sgpc.gov.sg), since every mas.gov.sg path tried this session -- including the Project Orchid page itself and this release's own mas.gov.sg URL -- serves an identical 853,817-byte HTML page titled 'Maintenance' rather than the document; per the project's named-entity rule, this card does not name the release's own Annex A list of Project Orchid industry-group members or the named participants in its four newly announced trials

MAS media release, 'MAS Announces Successful Live Trial of Settlement of Interbank Overnight Lending Using Wholesale Central Bank Digital Currency', Singapore, 13 November 2025 -- announces the successful completion of a live trial settling interbank overnight-lending transactions using wholesale CBDC on the Singapore Dollar Test Network ('SGD Testnet'), described in the release as 'an operational shared ledger infrastructure that enables financial institutions...to test the settlement of tokenised financial assets using wholesale CBDC', and stating this trial 'featured the first live issuance of Singapore dollar wholesale CBDC, with transactions recorded in the banks' official books and regulatory filings'; sets out SGD Testnet's three core functionalities -- a common settlement asset (issuance, transfer and redemption of SGD wholesale CBDC to settle tokenised-asset transactions), programmability (conditional logic tied to counterparty payment arrangements), and multi-asset atomic settlement (of cash and securities components together); states MAS 'will build on this CBDC settlement pilot, and plans in a future trial, to issue tokenised MAS Bills to Primary Dealers which will be settled using CBDC', with 'more details of this future trial...shared in 2026'; confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF via sgpc.gov.sg, since the equivalent mas.gov.sg release URL served the identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' block on live fetch this session; per the named-entity rule, this card does not name the release's own list of participating financial institutions

Project Orchid -- MAS's own multi-year, multi-phase exploratory programme on the design and technical aspects of digital money in Singapore, per its description within the two media releases above; this session could not independently re-confirm the programme's current scope or status via direct fetch of MAS's own Project Orchid page (mas.gov.sg/schemes-and-initiatives/project-orchid) or the Orchid Blueprint PDF itself (orchid-blueprint-final.pdf), both of which returned the identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' bot-block on live fetch this session -- this card's description of the Blueprint's infrastructure building blocks is therefore drawn entirely from the 16 November 2023 media release's own summary of the Blueprint, not from the Blueprint document itself -- see open_items

IRAS e-Tax Guide, 'GST: Digital Payment Tokens' (Third Edition, published 30 January 2026; first edition 19 November 2019; second edition 3 August 2022) -- confirmed by direct fetch of the current PDF from iras.gov.sg; states verbatim that '[w]ith effect from 1 Jan 2020, supplies of digital payment tokens will no longer be subject to GST', specifically that using a digital payment token as payment gives rise to no taxable supply of the token itself, and that exchanging a digital payment token for fiat currency or other digital payment tokens (or lending/advancing/crediting one) is an exempt supply that does not count toward the GST-registration turnover threshold; this is the money-adjacent tax fact for this pillar -- a general-taxation rule administered by IRAS under the GST Act, not a monetary-policy or central-banking measure administered by MAS -- registered here because it is one of the few Singapore official-domain sources genuinely fetchable this session (alongside sgpc.gov.sg and parliament.gov.sg); full treatment of this e-Tax Guide's definitional boundary between a GST-exempt 'digital payment token' and a currency-pegged stablecoin is tracked in the Stablecoins pillar and not repeated here to avoid duplicating citations
standing_summary: Singapore has no statute dedicated to banking- or central-bank-money-related digital-asset activity: this pillar covers how the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) -- acting in its central-bank capacity, distinct from its role administering the Payment Services Act 2019 and Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 licensing regimes covered in this jurisdiction's other pillars -- is developing wholesale central bank digital currency (CBDC) and exploring tokenised bank liabilities through its own convened programmes and live pilots rather than through new legislation, plus the one genuinely money-adjacent tax fact (IRAS's GST treatment of digital payment tokens) that touches this pillar from outside MAS's own regulatory perimeter. On 16 November 2023, MAS announced three initiatives in a single media release, confirmed by direct fetch via the Singapore Government's official press-release repository (sgpc.gov.sg) since mas.gov.sg itself -- including this release's own URL and MAS's Project Orchid page -- served an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' bot-block page to every path tried this session: publication of the 'Orchid Blueprint', a technology-infrastructure design for digital money built on four components (a settlement ledger, a tokenisation bridge linking account-based settlement systems to tokenised-money ledgers, a 'Purpose Bound Money' (PBM) programmability protocol, and a wallet-address Name Service); expansion of Project Orchid's industry trials, including one exploring tokenised bank liabilities usable across different issuing banks; and a plan to begin developing wholesale CBDC for interbank settlement the following year, with MAS piloting the 'live' issuance of wholesale CBDC for the first time, having previously only simulated issuance in test environments under the earlier Project Ubin (whose simulated wholesale CBDC was, per this release's own footnote, 'not available to the general public'). The release states MAS is promoting three distinct forms of digital money side by side -- 'wholesale CBDCs, tokenised bank liabilities, and regulated stablecoins' -- defining a wholesale CBDC as a token 'issued only to financial institutions for the settlement of inter-bank transactions' and representing 'a direct liability of the central bank', as distinct from a tokenised bank liability, which represents 'a commercial bank's liability' rather than a central-bank one; regulated stablecoins themselves are tracked in full in the Stablecoins pillar and not restated here. Then-MAS Managing Director Ravi Menon is quoted describing 'live' central-bank digital-money issuance as 'a significant milestone in MAS' digital money journey that began in 2016'. That plan reached a confirmed milestone on 13 November 2025: MAS's own media release, again fetched as a genuine PDF via sgpc.gov.sg rather than the blocked mas.gov.sg, announces the successful completion of a live trial settling interbank overnight-lending transactions using wholesale CBDC on the Singapore Dollar Test Network ('SGD Testnet'), which it describes as 'an operational shared ledger infrastructure that enables financial institutions...to test the settlement of tokenised financial assets using wholesale CBDC'; the release states this trial 'featured the first live issuance of Singapore dollar wholesale CBDC, with transactions recorded in the banks' official books and regulatory filings' -- MAS's own language distinguishing this from the earlier, purely simulated Project Ubin issuance. The release sets out SGD Testnet's three core functionalities as MAS itself describes them: a common settlement asset (issuance, transfer and redemption of SGD wholesale CBDC to settle tokenised-asset transactions, intended to reduce settlement risk and market fragmentation), programmability (conditional logic tied to counterparty payment agreements for real-time execution), and multi-asset atomic settlement (of cash and securities components together, eliminating the settlement risk that arises when the two legs settle through different systems or at different times). MAS states it 'will build on this CBDC settlement pilot, and plans in a future trial, to issue tokenised MAS Bills to Primary Dealers which will be settled using CBDC', with further details 'to be shared in 2026' -- as of this card's generation date (13 July 2026), this research found no MAS primary release confirming that further trial has yet occurred; secondary trade-press reporting describing a '2026 tokenised CBDC pilot' was not independently corroborated against a MAS primary source this session and is not asserted here as settled fact (see open_items). Consistent with the named-entity rule, this pillar describes both pilots' regulatory and infrastructural scope only, without naming the specific financial institutions either media release itself names as participants. Separately, and outside MAS's own perimeter, IRAS's e-Tax Guide 'GST: Digital Payment Tokens' (Third Edition, published 30 January 2026, directly fetched from iras.gov.sg) states that, '[w]ith effect from 1 Jan 2020, supplies of digital payment tokens will no longer be subject to GST' -- using a digital payment token as payment for goods or services gives rise to no taxable supply of the token, and exchanging one for fiat currency or another digital payment token (or lending, advancing or crediting one) is treated as an exempt supply that does not count toward the GST-registration turnover threshold. This is the one genuinely money-adjacent tax fact touching this pillar: a general-taxation rule under the GST Act, administered by IRAS rather than MAS, with no bearing on monetary policy, CBDC issuance, or bank prudential regulation, and included here -- rather than only in the Stablecoins pillar, where this e-Tax Guide's stablecoin-specific definitional boundary is tracked in full -- because it is IRAS's clearest primary statement of a fact that is 'money-adjacent' in the plain sense of the term. This research did not locate, and does not assert, any MAS primary statement on retail CBDC (a 'digital Singapore dollar') dated within this session's reach: MAS's own 2022 parliamentary-reply page on that topic (mas.gov.sg/news/parliamentary-replies/2022/reply-to-cos-cut-on-digital-sing-dollar), which trade press has long cited for MAS's 'no pressing need' framing, returned the identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' block on live fetch this session, so this card makes no claim -- current or historical -- about MAS's retail-CBDC position; the two live-confirmed media releases above concern wholesale CBDC and tokenised bank liabilities only, not a retail digital SGD. This pillar covers MAS's central-bank digital-money programme and the IRAS GST fact only; MAS's broader tokenisation work convened under Project Guardian, the Global Layer One initiative, and the Guardian Funds Framework is tracked in the Tokenization & RWA and Funds & ETFs pillars, and the BLOOM (Borderless, Liquid, Open, Online, Multi-currency) initiative's industry-collaboration work on tokenised bank liabilities and regulated stablecoins as settlement instruments is tracked in the Stablecoins pillar -- none of these is restated here to avoid duplicating citations across pillars.

key_links.label: Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Lays Foundation for Safe and Innovative Use of Digital Money in Singapore' (16 Nov 2023) -- Orchid Blueprint publication, expanded Project Orchid trials, wholesale CBDC development plan; official PDF, fetch-verified live this session

Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Announces Successful Live Trial of Settlement of Interbank Overnight Lending Using Wholesale Central Bank Digital Currency' (13 Nov 2025) -- first live issuance of SGD wholesale CBDC on the SGD Testnet; official PDF, fetch-verified live this session
IRAS e-Tax Guide: GST -- Digital Payment Tokens (Third Edition, published 30 Jan 2026) -- GST exemption for DPT supplies from 1 Jan 2020
MAS: Project Orchid (schemes-and-initiatives page) -- link only; live-verified this session as blocked, serving an 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' page instead of the page content

MAS: 'MAS Announces Successful Live Trial of Settlement of Interbank Overnight Lending Using Wholesale Central Bank Digital Currency' (mas.gov.sg original URL) -- link only; live-verified this session as blocked, serving the same 'Maintenance' page; listed for reference only, not used as a fetch source

open_items: MAS's general central-banking statute (the Monetary Authority of Singapore Act, Cap. 186) was not independently fetched this session -- sso.agc.gov.sg (Singapore Statutes Online, registered in config/jurisdictions/sg.json as the 'sg_legislation' regulator entry) returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried in prior sessions -- so this card does not cite any specific MAS Act provision as the statutory basis for MAS's wholesale-CBDC or Project Orchid work. It treats that work as central-bank policy initiative and pilot activity, consistent with there being no dedicated banking-money statute or regulation in force, rather than asserting an unconfirmed statutory hook.

As of this card's generation date (13 July 2026), MAS's own 13 November 2025 media release states only that further detail on a tokenised-MAS-Bills trial with Primary Dealers, settled using wholesale CBDC, would be 'shared in 2026'. This research did not find a MAS primary release confirming that trial has since occurred or been formally announced with specifics; secondary trade-press reporting describing a '2026 tokenised CBDC pilot' was not independently corroborated against a MAS primary source this session and is not treated as confirmed. Should be re-checked against MAS's media-releases index once genuinely fetchable, or via a manually supplied URL per this jurisdiction's manual-assisted-watcher arrangement.

This card makes no claim about MAS's position on a retail central bank digital currency ('digital Singapore dollar'). MAS's 2022 parliamentary-reply page on that topic, long cited by secondary sources for a 'no pressing need' framing, returned the same live-verified 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' block as every other mas.gov.sg path tried this session, and no sgpc.gov.sg mirror of that specific parliamentary reply was located (unlike MAS's media releases, which are consistently mirrored there). The two primary sources this card does rely on (16 Nov 2023 and 13 Nov 2025 media releases) concern wholesale CBDC and tokenised bank liabilities only, not retail CBDC; this omission is deliberate, not an oversight.

MAS's own Project Orchid page and the Orchid Blueprint PDF itself (orchid-blueprint-final.pdf) could not be independently fetched this session -- both mas.gov.sg paths returned the identical 'Maintenance' bot-block. This card's account of the Blueprint's four infrastructure building blocks (settlement ledger, tokenisation bridge, PBM programmability protocol, Name Service) is drawn entirely from the 16 November 2023 media release's own summary of the Blueprint, not from independent reading of the Blueprint document itself, and should be re-verified against the primary Blueprint PDF once mas.gov.sg is fetchable.

Project Ubin, the predecessor simulated-wholesale-CBDC programme referenced in the 16 November 2023 release's own footnote 5, is not independently sourced in this card beyond that footnote; no dedicated Project Ubin primary source was fetched this session.

The IRAS e-Tax Guide's definitional boundary between a GST-exempt 'digital payment token' and a currency-pegged stablecoin (i.e. whether a formally issuer-pegged stablecoin falls outside the DPT category for GST purposes) is tracked in full, including the relevant open question about whether the PS Act's own DPT definition mirrors the GST Act's, in the Stablecoins pillar; this card states only the GST-exemption fact itself as the money-adjacent tax point and does not restate that boundary analysis.

Tokenised bank liabilities as a settlement instrument are also referenced in MAS's 16 October 2025 BLOOM initiative media release (fetched and cited in full in the Stablecoins pillar), which describes MAS convening industry participants to standardise settlement using 'tokenised bank liabilities and well-regulated stablecoins'. This card does not restate BLOOM's scope or citation to avoid duplicating it across pillars; readers interested in the industry-collaboration dimension of tokenised bank liabilities should consult the Stablecoins pillar.

config/site.json's registry entry for Singapore (jurisdiction id 'sg') currently lists config as null, even though config/jurisdictions/sg.json exists on disk and is fully populated with regulator entries (mas, sg_legislation, sgpc, iras, sg_parliament) used as citation sources by this card and its sibling pillar cards. This appears to be a stale registry pointer rather than a missing config file; flagged here for visibility only -- config/site.json is outside the AI path allowlist (content/ and data/ only) and this card does not and cannot modify it.
